question: [File: 'File Name: pinger/mirror_node_client.go -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: fetchMirrorNodeNetwork()] Can an unprivileged external attacker exploit the fact that hiero.AccountIDFromString(n.NodeAccountID) errors are silently ignored (continue on error) to inject nodeEntry records with malformed NodeAccountIDs alongside valid entries with attacker-controlled ServiceEndpoints, causing the network map to be populated exclusively with attacker-controlled gRPC endpoints that receive operator-signed transactions without owner approval?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/mirror_node_client.go -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: fetchMirrorNodeNetwork()] Can an unprivileged external attacker who controls a malicious mirror node return a nodesEnvelope with a single nodeEntry whose ServiceEndpoints all point to the attacker's gRPC server, causing the pinger's network map to contain only attacker-controlled endpoints, so that every operator-signed CryptoTransfer transaction is submitted exclusively to attacker infrastructure without owner approval?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/mirror_node_client.go -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: fetchMirrorNodeNetwork()] Can an unprivileged external attacker exploit the absence of source IP validation on the mirror node HTTP response to perform a BGP hijacking attack that redirects traffic destined for the mirror node to an attacker-controlled server, which returns a crafted nodesEnvelope causing the pinger to route all operator-signed transactions to attacker nodes without signer approval?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/mirror_node_client.go -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: buildNetworkFromMirrorNodes()] Can an unprivileged external attacker exploit the fact that buildNetworkFromMirrorNodes() is called once at startup (via newClient() in sdk_client.go) with no periodic re-validation of the network map, meaning that if the attacker successfully injects a malicious network map at startup, all subsequent operator-signed transactions will be routed to attacker-controlled nodes indefinitely without owner approval?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/mirror_node_client.go -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: fetchMirrorNodeNetwork()] Can an unprivileged external attacker exploit the strings.TrimSpace(ep.DomainName) call that only trims whitespace but does not validate the domain name format, injecting a DomainName value containing special characters (e.g., '@', '/', '#') that are interpreted by net.JoinHostPort() or the gRPC dialer in unexpected ways, causing connection to attacker-controlled infrastructure where signed transactions are submitted without owner approval?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/mirror_node_client.go -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: fetchMirrorNodeNetwork()] Can an unprivileged external attacker exploit the strings.TrimSpace(ep.IPAddressV4) call that only trims whitespace but does not validate that the value is actually an IPv4 address, injecting a hostname or IPv6 address in the IPAddressV4 field that resolves to attacker-controlled infrastructure, causing operator-signed transactions to be submitted to attacker nodes without owner approval?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/mirror_node_client.go -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: fetchMirrorNodeNetwork()] Can an unprivileged external attacker exploit the network map construction (network[addr] = nodeAccountId) where the same addr key can be overwritten by multiple nodeEntry records, injecting duplicate entries where the last entry maps a legitimate gRPC address to an attacker-controlled AccountID, causing the SDK to associate signed transactions with incorrect node accounts without owner approval?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/mirror_node_client.go -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: fetchMirrorNodeNetwork()] Can an unprivileged external attacker who controls a malicious mirror node return a nodesEnvelope with serviceEndpoint.Port set to a privileged port number (e.g., 80, 443, 22) pointing to attacker-controlled services, bypassing the only port check (ep.Port == 0) and causing the pinger to connect to attacker infrastructure where operator-signed transactions are submitted without owner approval?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/mirror_node_client.go -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: fetchMirrorNodeNetwork()] Can an unprivileged external attacker exploit the absence of HTTPS enforcement on the mirror node HTTP client (http.Client with no Transport configured to require TLS) to perform a protocol downgrade from HTTPS to HTTP, intercepting the plaintext response and injecting malicious nodeEntry data that causes signed transactions to be routed to attacker-controlled gRPC endpoints without owner approval?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/mirror_node_client.go -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: buildNetworkFromMirrorNodes()] Can an unprivileged external attacker exploit the context cancellation path (ctx.Done() in the backoff select) to race the context cancellation with a malicious mirror node response, causing the pinger to partially initialize the network map with attacker-controlled entries before the context is cancelled, and then on restart use a stale or corrupted network map to route signed transactions without owner approval?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/mirror_node_client.go -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: fetchMirrorNodeNetwork()] Can an unprivileged external attacker exploit the fact that the http.Client.Timeout (cfg.mirrorNodeClientTimeout) is the only resource limit, with no limit on response body size, to send a slow-drip HTTP response that keeps the connection open for the full timeout duration while injecting malicious nodeEntry data byte-by-byte, eventually causing the pinger to accept a crafted network map and route signed transactions to attacker nodes without owner approval?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/mirror_node_client.go -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: fetchMirrorNodeNetwork()] Can an unprivileged external attacker who controls a malicious mirror node return a nodesEnvelope where nodeEntry.ServiceEndpoints contains an entry with DomainName set to 'localhost' or '127.0.0.1', causing the pinger to route operator-signed transactions to the local loopback interface, potentially interacting with local services and authorizing transactions without the legitimate network's involvement?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/mirror_node_client.go -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: fetchMirrorNodeNetwork()] Can an unprivileged external attacker exploit the absence of validation that returned NodeAccountIDs correspond to known Hedera consensus nodes, injecting a nodesEnvelope where attacker-controlled gRPC endpoints are mapped to arbitrary AccountIDs (e.g., 0.0.999999), causing the SDK to submit operator-signed transactions to attacker nodes under the guise of legitimate node accounts without owner approval?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/mirror_node_client.go -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: fetchMirrorNodeNetwork()] Can an unprivileged external attacker exploit the fact that the network map returned by fetchMirrorNodeNetwork() is passed directly to hiero.ClientForNetwork(netmap) in sdk_client.go without any cross-validation against a hardcoded or trusted node list, causing the SDK client to be initialized with entirely attacker-controlled node endpoints that receive all subsequent operator-signed transactions without owner approval?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/mirror_node_client.go -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: fetchMirrorNodeNetwork()] Can an unprivileged external attacker who controls a malicious mirror node return a nodesEnvelope with serviceEndpoint entries where IPAddressV4 is set to a cloud metadata service IP (e.g., 169.254.169.254), causing the pinger's gRPC connections to probe the cloud metadata endpoint, potentially leaking credentials or instance metadata that could be used to further compromise the operator key and authorize transactions without owner approval?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/mirror_node_client.go -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: buildNetworkFromMirrorNodes()] Can an unprivileged external attacker exploit the URL suffix check (strings.HasSuffix(base, '/api/v1')) to craft a mirrorRest value that ends with '/api/v1' but points to an attacker-controlled server (e.g., 'http://attacker.com/api/v1'), causing the URL to be constructed as 'http://attacker.com/api/v1/network/nodes' and the pinger to fetch a malicious nodesEnvelope that routes signed transactions without owner approval?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/mirror_node_client.go -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: fetchMirrorNodeNetwork()] Can an unprivileged external attacker exploit the absence of response Content-Type validation in fetchMirrorNodeNetwork() to inject a response with a non-JSON Content-Type that still contains valid JSON, or conversely a JSON Content-Type with malicious content, bypassing any content-type-based security controls and causing json.NewDecoder to parse attacker-controlled node data that routes signed transactions without owner approval?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/mirror_node_client.go -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: fetchMirrorNodeNetwork()] Can an unprivileged external attacker exploit the fact that resp.Body is read without an io.LimitReader wrapper, sending a response body of gigabytes of JSON data that causes the pinger process to exhaust available memory, potentially causing a crash-restart cycle where the pinger re-fetches the network map from an attacker-controlled mirror node and initializes with malicious endpoints that route signed transactions without owner approval?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/mirror_node_client.go -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: fetchMirrorNodeNetwork()] Can an unprivileged external attacker who controls a malicious mirror node return a nodesEnvelope with a nodeEntry where NodeAccountID is set to the operator's own account ID (e.g., 0.0.2), causing the SDK to route transactions to an attacker-controlled gRPC endpoint associated with the operator's account, enabling the attacker to intercept and replay operator-signed transactions without owner approval?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/mirror_node_client.go -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: fetchMirrorNodeNetwork()] Can an unprivileged external attacker exploit the silent error handling for hiero.AccountIDFromString() failures (errors are ignored with continue) to inject a mix of valid and invalid NodeAccountID entries, where valid entries all map to attacker-controlled ServiceEndpoints, ensuring the network map is populated exclusively with attacker-controlled gRPC addresses that receive operator-signed transactions without owner approval?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/mirror_node_client.go -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: buildNetworkFromMirrorNodes()] Can an unprivileged external attacker exploit the fact that the http.Client created in buildNetworkFromMirrorNodes() follows HTTP redirects by default (Go's http.Client follows up to 10 redirects), allowing a malicious mirror node to issue a redirect to an attacker-controlled URL that returns a crafted nodesEnvelope, causing the pinger to build a network map from attacker-controlled data and route signed transactions without owner approval?
